text: Intercostal hernias are a relatively rare clinical entity. Approximately thirty cases have been reported in the global literature. These are referred to as abdominal intercostal hernias if no diaphragmatic injury has occurred. These are typically secondary to trauma or previous surgery. They are more common in men and more common on the left side of the abdomen. abdominal intercostal hernias typically occur inferior to the ninth rib. Patients who develop these hernias after non-traumatic events, such as coughing or straining, typically have an underlying disorder leading to connective tissue weakness. Our patient is a fifty-three-year-old male who presented to the office with worsening right-sided abdominal and chest wall pain. Several months prior, he had a respiratory infection leading to excessive coughing. One particular episode of forceful coughing was followed by right-sided flank ecchymosis. The patient described an enlarging mass of the right inferior chest wall. He had associated pain with most physical activity and his pain was slightly improved with rest. He also reported intermittent bowel movement changes. He had a medical history significant for obesity, coronary artery disease, diabetes, and initially presented with a BMI of thirty seven point six.

Physical examination demonstrated a large, approximately fifteen by twenty centimeter defect of the right flank between ribs nine and ten, without palpable fascial margins. The patient was instructed to lose weight and to use an abdominal binder in the interim for symptomatic control. A preoperative CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was obtained. The patient's CT scan demonstrated a large defect between ribs nine and ten on the right side, with protrusion of the right liver and hepatic flexure through the defect. No other gross abnormalities were noted.

The surgeon uses a robotic-assisted approach to repair the large intercostal hernia. Minimal dissection is performed to create space between the right colon, right liver, and abdominal wall. A large piece of coated polypropylene mesh is inserted and oriented within the abdomen, with several stay sutures placed circumferentially to anchor the mesh to the abdominal wall.

The surgeon uses a running self-retaining absorbable suture to secure the mesh circumferentially, starting with the posterior location. The mesh is sewn completely around, and additional anchoring sutures are placed to prevent displacement or migration.

The patient underwent an uneventful surgery and was discharged to home on post-operative day one. The patient recovered well and that outpatient follow-up had resolved pain and discomfort. As of six months, the patient has not had any recurrent symptoms or recurrent hernia. While a rare entity, intercostal hernias may be successfully repaired through a minimally invasive robotic-assisted approach. The rarity of intercostal hernias makes dedicated study difficult, but minimally invasive approaches may allow for symptom resolution without the higher risk of morbidity associated with open repair.
